summary:
The Indo-Scythian Kingdom (c. 200 BC – 400 AD) was the state established by the Saka ([[event:1172|Saka Kingdoms]]) in northwestern India after they displaced the [[event:1104|Indo-Greek Kingdom]], controlling the Indus Valley and parts of the northwestern subcontinent and leaving a lasting legacy in Indian calendrical tradition through the Saka Era.

The Saka (Scythians) moved into Arachosia and the Indus region after being displaced from Central Asia by the [[event:1174|Yuezhi Migration]] around 130–100 BC. Their early rulers bore Iranian names but quickly adopted Indian cultural practices, issuing bilingual coins (Greek and Kharosthi), patronizing Buddhism, and governing through local satraps. The Great Satraps of Mathura and the Western Satraps of Gujarat became the most enduring Saka political entities. Their presence in India contributed to the distinctive Gandharan cultural synthesis. The Saka Era calendar — beginning in 78 AD — remains the official civil calendar of independent India, a remarkable survival of Scythian political heritage in modern South Asian culture. The Saka kingdoms were eventually absorbed by the Gupta Empire in the 4th century AD.
